coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots embedded hydrogel spheres for sensing and removing rifampicin
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yunhe
Mou, Chengjian
Xie, Zhigang
Zheng, Min
coredata.dc:description: Although a plethora of carbon dots (CDs) based sensors continue to spring up, hardly any study on CDs acting as fluorescent probe for detecting rifampicin (RF) has been reported so far. Furthermore, no CDs hydrogel spheres (HSs) were fabricated and utilized as optical sensors as well as absorbent to recognize and remove pollutants. In this paper, bright blue luminescent CDs possess remarkable fluorescence sensing ability for the detection of RF, with high selectivity, outstanding sensitivity (LOD, 0.46 μM) and good anti-interference, which enabled CDs to successfully applied for the quantitative determination of RF in different real water samples. Furthermore, in order to integrate the advantages of hydrogel and CDs, fluorescent CDs HSs were fabricated, which could not only act as efficient optical sensors to recognize RF, but also conduct as absorbent to facilitate RF removal. The present work offers a new insight into taking advantage of CDs for the detection of RF in a convenient and efficient way.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hydrogel spheres
Fluorescent probe
Rifampicin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The exploration of CDs as sensors
Anti interference experiment of RF detection
The mechanism of CDs for RF detection
Detection of RF in real samples
Detection of RF by CDs hydrogel spheres (HSs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDs. (b) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs. (c) The excitation and maximum emission spectra of CDs. (d) The emission spectra of CDs under different excitation wavelengths.

(a) The total spectrum of XPS. (b) C1s, (c) N1s and (d) O1s high-resolution XPS spectra of CDs. (e) The effect of pH on the emission of CDs. (f) The fluorescence changes of CDs under the irradiation of UV light (365 nm).

(a) Fluorescence titration curves of CDs with RF. (b) The photographs of CDs under UV (365 nm) light upon the addition of RF. (c) The linear fitting curve of fluorescence intensity of CDs at 480 nm vs [RF].

(a) Selectivity of CDs toward RF. (b) Effect of co-existing substances (3 NaCl, 4 KCl, 5 NH4F, 6 Na2SO4, 7 L-Cys, 8 L-GA, 9 L-Arg, 10 AA, 11 urea, 12 Glu, 13 Tet, 14 Cd2+, 15Fe2+, 16 Fe3+, 17 Cu2+) on the detection of RF.
(a) The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of RF. (b) The fluorescence decay curves of CDs and CDs + RF.

Photos of CDs HSs under (a) natural light and (b) UV light (365 nm). (c) Fluorescent photos of CDs HSs after soaking in different concentrations of RF solution (25, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 175 and 200 μM). (d) The emission intensity of CDs HSs at 480 nm vs the concentration of RF. (e) The maximum absorption capacity of HSs in various concentrations of RF solution. (f) The photos of RF solution before and after being absorbed by CDs HSs.
The results of CDs detecting RF in real water samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots embedded hydrogel spheres for sensing and removing rifampicin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin, 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin, 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although a plethora of carbon dots (CDs) based sensors continue to spring up, hardly any study on CDs acting as fluorescent probe for detecting rifampicin (RF) has been reported so far. Furthermore, no CDs hydrogel spheres (HSs) were fabricated and utilized as optical sensors as well as absorbent to recognize and remove pollutants. In this paper, bright blue luminescent CDs possess remarkable fluorescence sensing ability for the detection of RF, with high selectivity, outstanding sensitivity (LOD, 0.46 μM) and good anti-interference, which enabled CDs to successfully applied for the quantitative determination of RF in different real water samples. Furthermore, in order to integrate the advantages of hydrogel and CDs, fluorescent CDs HSs were fabricated, which could not only act as efficient optical sensors to recognize RF, but also conduct as absorbent to facilitate RF removal. The present work offers a new insight into taking advantage of CDs for the detection of RF in a convenient and efficient way.

ce:section.ce:para: Rifampicin (RF) is a broad-spectrum antibacterial drug and is mainly used to treat tuberculosis and the infections caused by pathogenic bacteria. Nevertheless, its abuse and improper use generally brings about serious adverse effects, drug resistance and environmental pollution. Thus, establishing sensitive and efficient assays for detecting RF is of great concern. Although various methods have emerged to determine RF such as high-performance liquid chromatography [1] and electrochemistry [2], their shortcomings of expensive equipments, complicated sample preparation procedures and tedious processing time make it impossible to realize real-time online monitoring RF. By contrast, fluorometric method has attracted considerable attention due to the fast response and ease of operation. Recently, some nanomaterials including Cu [3] or Au nanoclusters [4], CdTe/ZnS quantum dots [5], and rare-earth doped upconversion micromaterials [6] were utilized as fluorescent sensors for probing RF, unfortunately, the disadvantages of intrinsic toxicity and complex synthetic procedures hinder their potential applications.

Carbon dots (CDs) are novel fluorescent carbon nanomaterials and have outstanding merits of distinct optical properties [7–9], good water solubility [10], excellent biocompatibility [11], and high stability [12], which exhibit great applications in bioimaging [13–17], sensing [18–23], and disease diagnosis and therapy [24,25]. So far, CDs have been successfully used to sense cations [26–30], organic pollutants [31–33], bioactive molecules [34–36] and antibiotics [37–39]. However, to our knowledge, there is rare report on CDs acting as fluorescent probe for detecting RF. On the other hand, hydrogels are environmentally friendly three-dimensional soft materials with the advantages of large porosity and excellent flexibility, which endow them with potential application in removing pollutants [40,41]. Thus, it will be a wise way to integrate the superior photoluminescence character of CDs and unique adsorption performance of hydrogel by way of incorporating CDs into the network of hydrogel. The CDs-based hydrogel can act as a convenient and portable sensor for on-site detection as well as promising absorbent for contaminants removal.

In this work, citric acid, urea and ammonium fluoride were used as starting materials to synthesize CDs via solvothermal method. CDs manifested high selectivity on RF with high sensitivity and strong anti-interference, and were successfully employed as fluorescent probes to sense RF in real water samples. In addition, fluorescent CDs hydrogel spheres (HSs) were constructed from CDs, sodium alginate, and calcium chloride. The intense blue fluorescence of CDs HSs enabled them as sensitive tools for the determination of RF. The absorption capacity of CDs HSs was conducive to remove RF from the environmental pollutants.

CDs were synthesized by solvothermal method with citric acid, urea and ammonia fluoride as starting materials and formamide as solvent [42]. Citric acid (480 mg), urea (1500 mg) and ammonium fluoride (46 mg) were completely dissolved in formamide (10 mL), then the solution was transferred to Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 4 h. After cooled to room temperature naturally, the obtained solution was added to 10 mL of NaOH (pH = 13) and stirred for 10 min. The mixture was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min. The precipitate was washed with HCl (pH = 2) and water for several times until the solution was neutral. After centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min, the product was obtained and dried at 60 °C.

Sodium alginate (1 g) was added to deionized water (70 mL), the mixture was heated at 80 °C with stirring to form viscous solution, and bubbles were removed by ultrasound. Then the mixture of sodium alginate solution (14.3 mg/mL, 10 mL) and CDs solution (1 mg/mL, 5 mL) was added to CaCl2 solution (100 mM, 5 mL), grey CDs hydrogel microspheres were formed quickly in 1 min.

Different volumes of RF solution was added to CDs solution to keep the final concentration of CDs constant as 25 μg/mL and the concentration of RF in the range of 5.0–110.0 μM. The fluorescence spectra were monitored under 400 nm excitation.

Different substance solution (sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), ammonia fluoride (NH4F), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), l-cysteine (L-Cys), l-glutamic acid (L-GA), l-arginine (L-Arg), ascorbic acid (AA), urea, and glucose (Glu), tetracycline (Tet), Cadmium nitrate (Cd2+), Ferrous nitrate (Fe2+), Ferric nitrate (Fe3+) and Copper nitrate (Cu2+), 20 mM, 10 μL) was added to the mixture (2 mL) of CDs (25 μg/mL) and RF (100 μM) solution. The fluorescence spectra of CDs + RF without or with interference ions were measured under 400 nm excitation.

Materials, instrumentation, effect of pH on fluorescence intensity of CDs, study on the stability of CDs, FT-IR spectrum of CDs, F1s high-resolution XPS spectrum of CDs, fluorescence lifetime of CDs and CDs + RF and the table of various approaches for RF assays were shown in the Supporting Information.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of CDs (Fig. 1 a) indicates that CDs are spherical nanoparticles with a diameter of 8–20 nm. CDs has three characteristic peaks at 318 nm (π-π* transition of CC), 414 nm (n-π* transition of CO) and 558 nm in the UV–vis region (Fig. 1b). The excitation spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1c) has two main peaks at 306 and 400 nm, which are consistent with the absorption bands of 318 and 414 nm. Under the excitation of 400 nm, the maximum emission of CDs aqueous solution is observed 480 nm. The emission spectra of CDs changed with excitation wavelength (Fig. 1d), and the optimal emission was at 480 nm.

The FT-IR spectrum of CDs displays the peaks at 3140 (N–H), 2790 (C–H), 1554 (CO–N), 1320 (C–O), 1200 (C–F), 1086 (C–N) and 697 cm−1 (C–O) (Fig. S1). There are four peaks in the full scan X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) of CDs: C1s (285.0 eV), N1s (398.7 eV), O1s (533.4 eV) and F1s (687.7 eV) (Fig. 2 a). The high-resolution XPS C1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) has three peaks at 284.5 (C–C/C–N), 286.0 (C–O) and 288.4 eV (CO/C–F). The peak at 399.1 eV in the N1s spectrum is attributed to C–N (Fig. 2c). The vibrations of C–O and CO in the O1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) are observed at 531.9 and 533.3 eV, respectively. A peak of F1s at 687.7 eV is ascribed to C–F (Fig. S2).

The effect of pH on the fluorescence of CDs was evaluated and depicted in Fig. 2e. The fluorescence intensity of CDs enhanced with the increase of pH from 3.0 to 8.0, indicating that CDs are pH sensitive. In the subsequent experiments, all of the CDs aqueous solution was pH 7.0. The photostability of CDs was studied by monitoring the fluorescence changes after the irradiation of UV light (365 nm). As shown in Fig. 2f, Although CDs were irradiated for 7 h, the fluorescence intensity decreased slightly, verifying that CDs possess high photostability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The exploration of CDs as sensors

ce:section.ce:para: The capability of CDs as nanoprobes to detect RF was investigated. The fluorescence intensity of CDs progressively decreased, upon gradually enhancing the concentration of RF (Fig. 3 a and b). The fluorescence of CDs was almost completely quenched, when 107.1 μM of RF was added. A Stern-Volmer plot was obtained between the emission intensity of CDs and [RF] in the range of 2.4–33.8 μM I0/I −1 = 0.05113[RF] - 0.09367, R2 = 0.981 (Fig. 3c), Where I0 and I is the emission intensity of CDs without or with RF. The LOD of RF was calculated to be 0.46 μM, indicating that CDs possess excellent analytical sensing ability for RF with high sensitivity. Furthermore, compared to other detection methods and fluorescence probes (Table S1), CDs have the superiority of environment friendly, low cost, high stability and easy preparation over other fluorescent sensors, which render them great potential for large-scale application in environmental surveillance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti interference experiment of RF detection
ce:section.ce:para: Good selectivity of a fluorescent probe is essential for practical application. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a, when CDs were treated with the diverse substrates (NaCl, KCl, NH4F, Na2SO4, L-Cys, L-GA, L-Arg, AA, urea, Glu, Tet, Cd2+, Fe2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+, 100 μM), the emission of CDs did not show remarkable changes, implying that CDs could behave as a fluorescent marker for recognizing RF. In the meantime, it is also vital for a sensor to distinguish the target substrate from other coexisting species. We investigated the emission changes of CDs toward RF along with various foreign substances. As depicted in Fig. 4b, no obvious change in the fluorescence intensity of CDs + RF was observed in the coexistence presence of competitive substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of CDs for RF detection
ce:section.ce:para: RF has three typical absorption peaks centered at 236, 332 and 472 nm (Fig. 5 a), the band at 472 nm overlaps well with the emission bands of CDs. Thus, the fluorescence quenching might origin from fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), where CDs act as a energy donor and RF behaves as an energy acceptor. The energy transfer from CDs to RF reduced the fluorescence lifetime of CDs, along with the bathochromic shift of emission [23,31]. The fluorescence lifetime of CDs + RF was 8.38 ns, which was shorter than that of CDs (8.72 ns) (Fig. 5b). Furthermore, the emission of CDs redshifted from 480 nm to 530 nm, as increasing the concentration of RF (Fig. 3a). These results were consistent with our previous study [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of RF in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The prominent selectivity and anti-interference capability of CDs provide a unique advantage for sensing RF in practical water samples. To verify the credibility and accuracy of the CDs-based probe for detecting real samples, recovery experiments were executed by dissolving different concentrations of RF in lake and tap water. As summarized in Table 1 , the recovery of RF in the lake water ranged from 105.1% to 119.7%, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) less than 4.06%. The recovery of RF in tap water ranged from 116.9% to 124.3%, with RSD less than 2.71%. These results demonstrated CDs could sense RF in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of RF by CDs hydrogel spheres (HSs)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of CDs HSs with an average diameter of 4 mm was described detailedly in the experimental section. CDs HSs was grey under natural light (Fig. 6 a), while they emitted bright blue fluorescence under UV light (Fig. 6b). When CDs HSs were soaked in different concentrations (25.0–200.0 μM) of RF solution for 1 h, the fluorescence of CDs HSs gradually weakened, and the color changed from intense blue to faint green (Fig. 6c), demonstrating that more and more RF was absorbed into CDs HSs. As depicted in Fig. 6d, the relationship between the fluorescence intensity of CDs HSs and [RF] could be linearly fitted to be I = 209.4-1.82 [RF] (0–50 μM, R2 = 0.988) and I = 146.6-0.51 [RF] (50–150 μM, R2 = 0.991), respectively, demonstrating that CDs HSs were potential candidates for monitoring the content of RF. Fig. 6e illustrated the concentration-dependent absorption of RF on CDs HSs, and the maximum absorption capacity of CDs HSs enhanced with increase of RF's concentration. As shown in Fig. 6f, after soaking CDs HSs in 200 μM of RF for 1 h, the color of RF aqueous solution changed from brown to yellow, implying that CDs HSs had satisfactory absorption performance.

In summary, CDs were prepared by utilizing citric acid, urea and ammonium fluoride as precursors and formamide as solvent through solvothermal method. CDs could effectively identify RF with a low detection limit (0.46 μM) and worked very well in quantitative determination of RF in real environmental samples with high recoveries (105.1%–124.3%). Particularly worth mentioning was that CDs HSs constructed from sodium alginate, CDs and calcium chloride exhibited intense blue emission and strong absorption capacity. CDs HSs could not only determine the concentration of RF but also remove RF from the environmental pollutants.

Yunhe Li: Methodology, Investigation, Software, Writing – original draft. Chengjian Mou: Methodology. Zhigang Xie: Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Min Zheng: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51873023), the Science and Technology Development Plan of Changchun City (No. 21ZY38).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2021.110023.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of rifampicin in human plasma by high-performance liquid chromatography coupled with ultraviolet detection after automatized solid-liquid extraction
Biomed Chromatogr

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Louveau B, Fernandez C, Zahr N, Sauvageon-Martre H, Maslanka P, Faure P, Mourah S, Goldwirt L. Determination of rifampicin in human plasma by high-performance liquid chromatography coupled with ultraviolet detection after automatized solid-liquid extraction. Biomed Chromatogr 2016; 30 (12):2009-2015.

Nanomaterials
